text: The Gutenberg press is often celebrated as a democratizing force — and it was. But Carr asks us to notice what the codex book, which preceded and outlasted Gutenberg, demanded of its reader: a posture of sustained surrender. Unlike the scroll, which was consulted in sections, the bound book invited cover-to-cover immersion. It created, over centuries, a new cognitive ideal: the contemplative reader, alone with a text, following a single thread of thought to its end. This ideal was cultural before it was neurological. The internet does not merely distract us from books; it replaces this cultural ideal with another — the networked browser, perpetually connected, perpetually partial.
question: How does the passage distinguish the codex book from the scroll in terms of what they demanded from readers?

options.text: The codex required literacy while the scroll could be interpreted through images
The codex invited cover-to-cover immersion while the scroll was consulted in sections
The codex was faster to read while the scroll required more deliberate pace
The codex was designed for private use while the scroll was read aloud publicly
